verses.text: Dans cette troisième série de discours, les amis de Job l'accusent explicitement de compter parmi les méchants et de partager leur sort.

Élifaz n'a pas compris que Dieu tirerait plaisir de la justice de Job ( **1.8 ** ; **2.3** ), comme de toute personne juste ( **1Ch 29.17 ** ; **Ps 147.10-11 ** ; **Pr 11.1** , **20 ** ; **12.22** ). Dieu tirerait également gloire de la foi inébranlable de Job, qui met en évidence le mensonge qui sous-tend le défi de Satan ( **Job 1.9-12 ** ; **2.4-6** ). • Job affirme avec constance qu'il est irréprochable ( **23.10-12** ), tout comme Dieu le dit de lui ( **1.1** , **8 ** ; **2.3** ).

Élifaz n'a pas compris que Dieu tirerait plaisir de la justice de Job ( **1.8 ** ; **2.3** ), comme de toute personne juste ( **1Ch 29.17 ** ; **Ps 147.10-11 ** ; **Pr 11.1** , **20 ** ; **12.22** ). Dieu tirerait également gloire de la foi inébranlable de Job, qui met en évidence le mensonge qui sous-tend le défi de Satan ( **Job 1.9-12 ** ; **2.4-6** ). • Job affirme avec constance qu'il est irréprochable ( **23.10-12** ), tout comme Dieu le dit de lui ( **1.1** , **8 ** ; **2.3** ).

22:4 Is the punishment inflicted on thee from fear of thee, in order to disarm thee? as Job had implied (see on Job 7:12; Job 7:20; and Job 10:17). will he enter . . . into judgment?— Job had desired this (Job 13:3, Job 13:21). He ought rather to have spoken as in Ps 143:2.

22:5 For thou hast taken a pledge from thy brother for nought,.... It can hardly be thought that it was for nothing at all, on no consideration whatever, or that nothing was lent, for which the pledge was taken; but that it was a small trifling sum, and comparatively nothing, not to be spoken of; or it was borrowed for so short a time, that there needed not any pledge it; and it was unkind to take it, especially of a brother, whether in nature, or in religion, whether a near kinsman, or friend, or neighbour.

Exiger des vêtements comme garantie à de l'argent prêté était interdit par la loi et condamné par les prophètes ( **Ex 22.26-27 ** ; **Dt 24.10-13 ** ; **Ez 18.7-8 ** ; **Am 2.8** ). En revanche, Job avait fourni des vêtements aux pauvres ( **Jb 31.19-20** ).

22:7 But as for the mighty man, he had the earth,.... A large share and portion of it, which Job could not hinder him from the enjoyment of, because mightier than he, or otherwise he would have done it; or Job was content he should have what he had, and gave him more than what of right belonged to him; for when any cause came before him as a judge, or civil magistrate, between a rich man, and a poorer man, relating to a field, or piece of land he always gave the cause to the rich and mighty and so he had the land, as is suggested: and the honourable man dwelt in it;

22:8 mighty—Hebrew, "man of arm" (Ps 10:15; namely, Job). honourable—Hebrew, "eminent, or, accepted for countenance" (Isa 3:3; Kg2 5:1); that is, possessing authority. Eliphaz repeats his charge (Job 15:28; so Zophar, Job 20:19), that it was by violence Job wrung houses and lands from the poor, to whom now he refused relief (Job 22:7, Job 22:9) [MICHAELIS].

L'Ancien Testament confie à Israël la responsabilité de prendre soin des veuves et des orphelins ( **Dt 10.18 ** ; **14.29** ) et condamne à plusieurs reprises leur négligence comme étant le pire des maux sociaux ( **Ex 22.22-24 ** ; **Dt 27.19** ). Job était d'accord pour dire que c'était mauvais ( **Jb 24.3** , **21** ) mais affirmait sa propre innocence ( **29.12-13 ** ; **31.16-18** , **21** ).

22:10 Or darkness, that thou canst not see,.... Or darkness is round about thee, thou art enveloped in it; meaning either judicial blindness, and darkness, and stupidity of mind, which must be his case, if he could not see the hand of God upon him, or the snares that were about him, or was not troubled with sudden fear; or else the darkness of affliction and calamity, which is often signified hereby, see Isa 8:22; afflictive dispensations of Providence are sometimes so dark, that a man cannot see the cause and reason of them, or why it is he is brought into them;

22:11 Is not God in the height of heaven?.... The heaven is high, it has its name from its height, and is noted for it; some of the heavens are higher than others, as the heaven of heavens, the third heaven, the habitation of angels and glorified saints; and here God dwells, this is the habitation of his holiness, and the high and holy place he inhabits; his throne is in heaven, in the heaven of heavens is his throne, where he in an especial manner manifests his glory, and the lustre of it;
Job ne prétendait pas que Dieu ne peut pas voir , mais d’autres l'avaient affirmé ( **Ps 10.11 ** ; **73.11 ** ; **94.7** ).
Job ne prétendait pas que Dieu ne peut pas voir , mais d’autres l'avaient affirmé ( **Ps 10.11 ** ; **73.11 ** ; **94.7** ).
Job ne prétendait pas que Dieu ne peut pas voir , mais d’autres l'avaient affirmé ( **Ps 10.11 ** ; **73.11 ** ; **94.7** ).
22:15 marked—Rather, Dost thou keep to? that is, wish to follow (so Hebrew, 2 Sam 22:22). If so, beware of sharing their end. the old way—the degenerate ways of the world before the flood (Gen 6:5).

22:16 cut down—rather, "fettered," as in Job 16:8; that is, arrested by death. out of time—prematurely, suddenly (Job 15:32; Ecc 7:17); literally, "whose foundation was poured out (so as to become) a stream or flood." The solid earth passed from beneath their feet into a flood (Gen 7:11).

22:17 Yet he filled their houses with good things,.... With temporal good things, with this world's good, with plenty of providential goodness; earthly enjoyments are good things in themselves, and in their effects, when rightly used, and these wicked men have their share of; this is their portion, they have their good things in this life, and a large abundance of them oftentimes; their hearts are filled with food, and should be with gladness and thankfulness; their bellies are filled with hidden treasures;
Élifaz a retourné à Job ses propres paroles ( **21.16b** ).

Ils seront heureux de voir la justice rendue et les injustices vengées (voir aussi **Ps 58.10-11 ** ; **Ap 19.1-4** ). Le sentiment d'Élifaz faisait fi du désir de Dieu de voir les pécheurs se repentir (voir **Ez 33.11** ).

Ils seront heureux de voir la justice rendue et les injustices vengées (voir aussi **Ps 58.10-11 ** ; **Ap 19.1-4** ). Le sentiment d'Élifaz faisait fi du désir de Dieu de voir les pécheurs se repentir (voir **Ez 33.11** ).

22:21 Eliphaz takes it for granted, Job is not yet "acquainted" with God; literally, "become a companion of God." Turn with familiar confidence to God. and be—So thou shalt be: the second imperatively expresses the consequence of obeying the first (Ps 37:27). peace—prosperity and restoration to Job; true spiritually also to us (Rom 5:1; Col 1:20). good— (1 Tim 4:8).

Le terme instructions (Hébreu torah, "loi", **Dt 17.11** ), qui n'est utilisé qu'ici dans Job, renvoie fréquemment au Pentateuque (Genèse-Deutéronome). • Voir **Dt 4.1-2 ** ; **6.6-9 ** ; **Ps 119.11 ** ; **Pr 2.1-9** .

Bildad ( **8.5-6** ) et Sofar ( **11.13-14** ) avaient déjà mal appliqué ce principe biblique ( **Es 55.6-7 ** ; **Os 14.1-2 ** ; **Za 1.3 ** ; **Ac 26.20** ), et voilà qu'Élifaz le faisait à son tour.

Ofir était probablement situé dans le sud de l'Arabie (voir **1R 9.28 ** ; **22.48** ). L'or d'Ophir était l'étalon de l'or pur de qualité ( **Es 13.12 ** ) ; les reines en portaient lors des mariages ( **Ps 45.9** ).
Dieu promet d'être le trésor de son peuple ( **Ps 16.5 ** ; **Es 33.6 ** ; **Mt 19.21 ** ; **Mc 10.21** ) ; bien qu'ils ne possèdent rien, ils seront riches ( **2Co 6.10 ** ; **Jc 2.5** ).

22:26 Thou shalt make thy prayer unto him,.... To God, and him only; for not a creature, angels, or men, are to be prayed to; and this is to be done in a supplicating, entreating way, as the word signifies; not pleading merit, but mercy, not presenting prayers and supplications for a man's own righteousness' sake, but for the Lord's mercy's sake, and for the sake of Christ and his righteousness; and prayer is to be made in this manner frequently, to be multiplied, as the word also signifies;

Paradoxalement, Job priera , Dieu écoutera , mais c'est Élifaz qui en tirera les bénéfices ( **42.8** ). • Les serments étaient souvent motivés par la repentance et le pardon ( **Os 14.2 ** ; **Jon 1.16 ** ; **2.9** ) et par la recherche d'une intervention divine ( **Jg 11.29-40 ** ; **1S 1.1-28** ). Les serments sont contraignants ( **Nb 30.2 ** ; **Dt 23.2 3** ; **Ps 22.25 ** ; **61.5** , **8 ** ; **116.14** , **18 ** ; **Ec 5.4-5** ).

22:28 When men are cast down,.... Wicked men are brought down from a state of prosperity to a state of adversity, are in low circumstances, great straits and difficulties: then thou shall say, there is lifting up; that is, for himself and his; when others are in adversity, he should be in prosperity; when others are cast down into a very low estate and distressed condition, he should be exalted to a very high estate, and be in affluent circumstances, see Ps 147:6;

Ce texte difficile exprime probablement l'efficacité de la prière d'un homme juste. Le principe ( **Ps 34.15** , **17 ** ; **145.18 ** ; **Pr 15.8** , **29 ** ; **Jc 5.16** ) en est illustré tout au long des Écritures (voir, par ex., **Gn 18.23-32 ** ; **19.29 ** ; **20.7** , **17 ** ; **32.28 ** ; **Ex 9.28-32 ** ; **17.11 ** ; **32.10-14 ** ; **Rm 5.19** ).

22:30 island—that is, "dwelling." But the Hebrew expresses the negative (1 Sam 4:21); translate "Thus He (God) shall deliver him who was not guiltless," namely, one, who like Job himself on conversion shall be saved, but not because he was, as Job so constantly affirms of himself, guiltless, but because he humbles himself (Job 22:29); an oblique attack on Job, even to the last. and it—Rather, "he (the one not heretofore guiltless) shall be delivered through the purity (acquired since conversion) of thy hands";
